title: starbucks-location-fitness-model
subtitle: GradientBoosting regressor predicting Starbucks location fitness from spatial, transit & competition features
description:
# Model Summary

A GradientBoosting regression model that predicts **Location Fitness Score (LFS)** for Starbucks store locations in Manhattan. LFS quantifies how well a location is positioned relative to demand drivers (transit, foot traffic) and competitive supply.

# Model Characteristics

- **Algorithm:** GradientBoostingRegressor (scikit-learn, 200 estimators)
- **Target:** `location_fitness_score` (demand_proxy_index / supply_index)
- **Features (21):** Building attributes (PLUTO), transit proximity & ridership (MTA), competition density, Census demographics, pedestrian counts, coordinates
- **Training data:** 171 Starbucks locations in Manhattan
- **Train/test split:** 80/20, random_state=42
- **Framework:** scikit-learn (joblib serialization)

# Data Overview

**Source:** [Manhattan Cafe Wars dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/shiratoriseto/manhattan-cafe-wars) (`stores_enriched_v4.csv`)

**Feature categories:**
| Category | Features |
|----------|----------|
| Building (PLUTO) | numfloors, yearbuilt, retailarea, comarea, lotarea, assesstot |
| Transit (MTA) | distance to station, avg daily ridership |
| Competition | Starbucks/Dunkin/other cafes within 500m, nearest competitor & Starbucks distance |
| Census (ACS) | population, median income, pct walk commute, pct bachelors+ |
| Pedestrian | nearest pedestrian count |
| Location | lat, lon |

**Leakage prevention:** `demand_proxy_index` and `supply_index` are explicitly excluded since LFS = demand / supply.

# Evaluation Results

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Test MAE | See notebook output |
| Test R² | See notebook output |
| CV method | 5-fold cross-validation |

**Top predictive features** (by permutation importance): transit ridership, competition density, and building attributes.

# Usage

```python
import joblib
model = joblib.load('lfs_model.joblib')
# model.predict(X) where X has 21 features in the documented order
```

# Limitations

1. Small dataset (n=171) — cannot support complex architectures
2. Single time snapshot — no temporal validation
3. Manhattan-specific — may not generalize to other cities
4. LFS is a derived metric, not ground truth (e.g., revenue)
